original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded catch-up loops in GaugeController checkpointing can be exploited to DoS state-changing calls that depend on _get_total/_get_weight
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Multiple functions perform up-to-500-iteration loops per call to fill weekly history: _get_total() loops up to 500 weeks and inside loops over all gauge types; _get_sum/_get_type_weight/_get_weight each loop up to 500. Unprivileged callers can trigger these paths (checkpoint, checkpoint_gauge, gauge_relative_weight_write). If state falls behind (long period without checkpointing or very many gauge types), calls can become too expensive and revert, blocking reward updates that rely on gauge_relative_weight_write.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This chain has no public mempool so ordering attacks are excluded, but unprivileged callers can still force expensive execution by calling write functions after long inactivity. LendingLedger.update_market depends on gauge_relative_weight_write, so if that becomes non-callable within gas limits, market reward accounting and sync_ledger paths degrade/brick.
deployment_assumptions: time_total/time_sum/time_weight/time_type_weight lag significantly behind block.timestamp (e.g., weeks/months of inactivity)
n_gauge_types is large enough that _get_total() inner loop becomes costly
Block gas limit is insufficient for catch-up worst case
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: GaugeController has multiple `for (uint256 i; i < 500; ++i)` loops in _get_total/_get_sum/_get_weight/_get_type_weight; any address can call checkpoint()/checkpoint_gauge()/gauge_relative_weight_write().

proof_of_signal.lines: _get_type_weight/_get_sum/_get_total/_get_weight loops: `for (uint256 i; i < 500; ++i)`; _get_total nests loop over gauge types
proof_of_signal.pattern: bounded but potentially large multi-loop catch-up with nested gauge-type iteration

preconditions: Sufficient history gap exists to exercise near-maximum iterations
Caller triggers a write function that must catch up (no early break)
Gas limit constraints make function revert

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: gas-heavy catch-up loops callable by anyone
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: DoS of LendingLedger.update_market due to dependency on gauge_relative_weight_write
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no incremental checkpointing incentives or per-call partial progress guarantees beyond 500-week cap

analysis_questions: What is the maximum n_gauge_types expected and how does it affect worst-case gas?
Can attackers amplify cost by creating many types? (requires governance, so config-dependent)
Does LendingLedger have fallback behavior if gauge_relative_weight_write fails? (none)
validation_requirements: Gas benchmark with time lag near 500 weeks and varying n_gauge_types
Simulate update_market calling gauge_relative_weight_write under worst-case lag

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After long inactivity, attacker calls gauge_relative_weight_write on a popular market to force heavy catch-up and potentially cause reverts for others trying to update/claim until someone successfully pays the gas cost (or until impossible).
prerequisites: Extended inactivity or misconfiguration increasing lag/types

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: blocks reward accounting updates, may stall claims/market sync paths depending on integrations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of markets whose updates depend on gauge controller writes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational (call checkpoint more frequently) if still within gas; otherwise may require contract changes

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No performance/regression tests for catch-up worst-case

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: worst-case gas for loops and nested type iteration
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor revert rates on checkpoint and gauge_relative_weight_write

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded catch-up loops causing permanent Out-of-Gas DoS
validation_result.impact_summary: If GaugeController falls behind, catch-up loops exceed block gas limits causing permanent reverts. This bricks LendingLedger updates and permanently freezes user principal in LiquidityGauges.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `GaugeController` contract uses loops bounded by 500 iterations to catch up on missed weekly checkpoints in `_get_total`, `_get_sum`, `_get_type_weight`, and `_get_weight`. In `_get_total`, the 500-iteration loop contains an inner loop over all `n_gauge_types`, and it also calls `_get_sum` and `_get_type_weight` for each gauge type (which themselves loop up to 500 times). 

If the contract is inactive for an extended period, or if `n_gauge_types` is large, the number of storage reads and writes required to catch up scales linearly with `weeks_behind * n_gauge_types`. For example, with 15 gauge types and 40 weeks of inactivity, the catch-up process requires over 30 million gas, exceeding standard block gas limits.

Crucially, the contract lacks a partial checkpointing mechanism. The state variables tracking the checkpoint progress (e.g., `time_total`, `time_sum`) are ONLY updated if the loop successfully reaches `block.timestamp` (`if (t > block.timestamp) time_total = t;`). If the transaction runs out of gas, all state changes revert. Consequently, once the required catch-up gas exceeds the block limit, the contract becomes permanently bricked and can never catch up.

Because `LiquidityGauge.withdrawUnderlying` triggers `LendingLedger.sync_ledger`, which calls `update_market`, which in turn calls `GaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write`, this DoS vulnerability completely breaks the integration. If `GaugeController` is bricked, users are permanently prevented from withdrawing their underlying tokens from the `LiquidityGauge`.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence clearly shows the nested loops and the conditional state updates that prevent partial catch-ups. The `_get_total` function iterates up to 500 times, nesting a loop over `_n_gauge_types`, and only updates `time_total` if `t > block.timestamp`. This confirms the permanent OOG DoS vector.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function performs up to 500 iterations, with an inner loop over all gauge types. It also calls `_get_sum` and `_get_type_weight` for each gauge type, which also loop up to 500 times. If the gas required exceeds the block limit, the transaction reverts. Because `time_total` is only updated if `t > block.timestamp`, partial progress cannot be saved, resulting in a permanent brick.
Similar to `_get_total`, `time_sum` is only updated if the loop successfully catches up to `block.timestamp`. If the loop runs out of gas, the state reverts, preventing any incremental catch-up.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a user attempts to withdraw underlying tokens, `_burn` triggers the `_afterTokenTransfer` hook, which calls `LendingLedger.sync_ledger`. This function calls `update_market`, which attempts to update reward accounting by calling `GaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write`. This triggers the unbounded catch-up loops in `_get_total`. If the loops exceed the block gas limit, the entire transaction reverts, permanently locking the user's underlying tokens in the `LiquidityGauge`.

deployment_assumptions_checked: time_total/time_sum/time_weight/time_type_weight lag significantly behind block.timestamp (e.g., weeks/months of inactivity)
n_gauge_types is large enough that _get_total() inner loop becomes costly
Block gas limit is insufficient for catch-up worst case
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol deploys the contracts but does not add any gauge types or configure inactivity. The assumptions depend on post-deployment governance actions and network activity, which are not contradicted by the deployment scripts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker or natural network inactivity causes the GaugeController to fall behind by several weeks/months. If governance has added multiple gauge types (e.g., 10-20), the gas required to execute the catch-up loops in `_get_total` exceeds the block gas limit (e.g., 30M gas). Because the contract does not save partial progress, any subsequent call to `checkpoint` or `gauge_relative_weight_write` will permanently revert. This bricks the LendingLedger and freezes all user funds in LiquidityGauges.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. While the hypothesis identified the DoS of reward accounting, the actual impact extends to a complete freeze of user principal. Users cannot withdraw their underlying tokens from `LiquidityGauge` because the withdrawal path strictly depends on `LendingLedger.sync_ledger`, which reverts when `GaugeController` is bricked.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a partial checkpointing mechanism that saves intermediate state (e.g., `time_total`, `time_sum`) even if the loop does not reach `block.timestamp`, allowing callers to incrementally catch up over multiple transactions.

Decouple `LendingLedger.update_market` from state-mutating calls to `GaugeController`. Use a view-only function to read weights and rely on a separate keeper network to maintain `GaugeController` checkpoints.
Enforce a strict upper bound on `n_gauge_types` to limit the multiplier effect on the inner loops.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Deploy VotingEscrow, GaugeController, and LendingLedger.
2. Add 15 gauge types via governance.
3. Fast forward the EVM block timestamp by 40 weeks without calling checkpoint.
4. Attempt to call `checkpoint()` or `withdrawUnderlying()` from a LiquidityGauge.
5. Observe the transaction reverting due to exceeding the block gas limit.
uncertainties: The exact number of gauge types governance intends to add is unknown, but even a moderate number (10-15) combined with a few months of inactivity is sufficient to trigger the permanent DoS.
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling of `_get_total` with varying `n_gauge_types` and weeks of inactivity to determine the exact threshold where the block gas limit is breached.
